text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

28.1	The Department of Global Communications is responsible for communicating to the world the ideals and work of the United Nations, interacting and partnering with diverse audiences, and building support for the purposes and principles enshrined in the Charter of the United Nations. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including resolutions 13 (I), 78/330 on multilingualism and 80/84 A and B on questions relating to information. The Department will leverage the power of communications to tell the United Nations story to global audiences in multiple languages and platforms to mobilize action in support of the United Nations agenda.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

28.2	The overall programme plan for 2027 reflects the Department’s strategy to strengthen support for the work and purposes of the United Nations by engaging with audiences and encouraging them to act, through impactful storytelling and a focus on solutions. The Department takes the following core approaches in its work: strategic planning by identifying communications goals and target audiences; maintaining close working relationships with stakeholders, offices of the Secretariat, entities of the United Nations system and other key partners; enhancing audience reach through multilingualism and the parity of all six official languages in the preparation of news and communications products, including content on United Nations websites and social media accounts; targeted use of social and digital media to deliver information directly and instantaneously to key audiences; and measuring the impact of campaigns in order to improve the Department’s work.

28.3	The Department delivers strategic communications campaigns to engage and inspire target audiences. It works through a global network of United Nations information centres, integrated with United Nations resident coordinator offices, to reach local audiences and provide content for global platforms. It produces accurate, objective and balanced multilingual news and communications products that convey authoritative information and solutions-based stories. It advocates on priority issues with Member States, civil society, academia, the media, young people and other partners. The Department contributes to the observance of international days and celebrations established by the General Assembly.

28.4	The Department also provides services to delegates and staff, including through the Dag Hammarskjöld Library, and promotes internal knowledge-sharing and communication by way of iSeek, the United Nations intranet.

28.5	The Department continues to place information integrity at the centre of its work and is committed to increasing access to relevant, reliable and accurate information and knowledge. In an age when misinformation, disinformation, hate speech and other content inciting harm are disseminated, the Department works towards advancing the United Nations Global Principles for Information Integrity and relevant commitments in the Pact for the Future and the Global Digital Compact throughout its work.

28.6	The Department will lead and coordinate crisis communications by strengthening the United Nations response through aligned messaging and mitigating risk by continuing to develop guidance on crisis communications.

28.7	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, the Department will leverage partnerships, including from the media, civil society, academia, the creative industry, the sports community and other representatives of the private sector, to enhance its ability to target and deliver information to an even wider community, with a focus on young people around the world.

28.8	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Department will strengthen the coordination of communications across the United Nations system, including through United Nations communications groups at the country and regional levels. Coordination will also be strengthened through the integration of United Nations information centres with resident coordinator offices and by streamlining processes and information-sharing. The Department will continue its work with the Development Coordination Office to provide strategic communications support to resident coordinators in countries without an information centre presence to ensure that information is widely disseminated.
28.9	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	All stakeholders and partners fulfil their responsibilities and obligations under service agreements and partnerships;
(b)	Access by target audiences to programmes, products and services is not limited by communications technology, security or infrastructure conditions.

28.10	The Department integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. For example, the Department will promote the balanced presence of women and men in all its products, campaigns, events and activities.

28.11	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Department will use its network of focal points to help to develop a common and coordinated approach to making United Nations services and activities accessible for persons with disabilities. The focal points will provide communications support to the Strategy, including by setting accessibility standards for United Nations websites.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 initiative

28.12	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects efforts of the programme to implement all mandates in a more efficient and effective manner and contains no substantive adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative. Pursuant to General Assembly resolutions 77/262, 78/330, 79/257 and 80/242, the Department is conducting an independent review of its operations and will provide the outcome of the review and any related restructuring for consideration by the Assembly during the main part of its eighty-first session.

28.13	The Department will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.
Legislative mandates
28.14	The list below, which was reviewed in the preparation of the proposed programme budget, provides all mandates entrusted to the programme.
General Assembly resolutions

children.text: Evaluation activities

28.16	As part of the independent review of the Department of Global Communications, the Department conducted an evaluation of its entire portfolio of websites in 2025, which has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

28.17	In response to the results of the evaluation referenced above, the Department is prototyping new digital products that reflect a streamlined user experience and better incorporate multimedia elements such as podcasts and live blogging.
28.18	An evaluation of the United Nations Academic Impact initiative is planned for 2027.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Campaigns and country operations services
Objective
28.19	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to mobilize audiences and strengthen support for the work, principles and purposes of the United Nations.
Strategy
28.20	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Deliver communications campaigns on United Nations priorities, including sustainable development, peace and security and human rights, directed by the United Nations global communications strategy and facilitated through a variety of communications tools, including audience-targeted messaging, issue-focused content and inspiring stories;
(b)	Promote these priorities through the United Nations information centres by developing multimedia content that supports targeted and localized messages and communications campaigns;
(c)	Provide strategic and crisis communications guidance to resident coordinator offices and United Nations country teams in their relevant regions;
(d)	Devise strategic guidance on information integrity and promote content to address false narratives and build confidence among global audiences.
28.21	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased knowledge and engagement of target audiences around the globe, and further action in support of United Nations priorities;
(b)	Global reach of communications campaigns available in the languages and on the platforms used by audiences, thus ensuring greater impact.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened public engagement on United Nations priority issues

28.22	In its resolution 80/84 B, the General Assembly stressed the primary mission of the Department in providing accurate, impartial, comprehensive, balanced, timely, relevant and multilingual information to the public on the tasks and responsibilities of the United Nations in order to strengthen international support for the activities of the Organization. To diversify the United Nations presence on various social media platforms amid the changing social media landscape and to reach wider audiences, especially in countries and regions with lower Internet bandwidth, the subprogramme opened a pilot WhatsApp channel in French in May 2024 that provided information on global priority issues and the work of the United Nations. By the end of 2024, the channel had gained more than half a million followers. Building on that success, in 2025 the subprogramme established additional channels in Arabic, English, Hindi and Portuguese, almost quadrupling follower numbers to 2.1 million by the end of 2025. United Nations information centres played a pivotal role in amplifying the reach and impact of those channels by actively promoting them through their local networks. They leveraged their on-the-ground presence to help to engage communities on global priority issues such as climate action, sustainable development, human rights and peace and security through WhatsApp channels in five languages. That innovative approach strengthened public engagement and fostered greater understanding of the work of the United Nations among diverse audiences worldwide.
28.23	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.I).
Figure 28.I
Performance measure: number of people engaged on United Nations priority issues through WhatsApp channels in several languages (annual)
(Millions)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: increased action on the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

28.24	The subprogramme’s work contributed to an additional 3.7 million individual actions logged towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, for a cumulative total of 28.2 million actions logged, exceeding the planned target of 26.5 million actions.
28.25	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.II).
Figure 28.II
Performance measure: number of individual actions logged through the ActNow campaign (cumulative)
(Millions)
Result 2: greater public understanding of and engagement on critical issues related to the Sustainable Development Goals – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

28.26	The subprogramme’s work contributed to a total of 37.4 million views of the Sustainable Development Goals and Climate Action websites in 2025, which did not meet the planned target of 60 million views. The target was not met owing to major shifts in public search behaviour in 2025, given that online search engines increasingly display summaries generated by artificial intelligence, which reduced traffic to external websites.
28.27	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.III).
Figure 28.III
Performance measure: number of views of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals and Climate Action websites (annual)
(Millions)
Result 3: expanded access to advocacy material and messaging on priority issues – Proposed programme plan for 2027

28.28	To ensure continued public access to information on priority issues amid evolving digital consumption patterns, the subprogramme strengthened its direct audience outreach in 2025 by launching the monthly “Hear Us” newsletter on peace and security. The subprogramme also continued to issue the Sustainable Development Goal “Goal of the Month” newsletter and the United Nations LinkedIn newsletter. These direct newsletter channels support the regular dissemination of advocacy material, messaging and calls to action on priority issues. During 2023 and 2024, the Sustainable Development Goal and LinkedIn newsletters built a combined subscriber base of more than 1.9 million, creating a strong foundation for direct engagement. In 2025, with the addition of the “Hear Us” newsletter, the combined subscriber base reached more than 2.3 million.
Lessons learned and planned change

28.29	The lesson for the subprogramme was that strengthening its own communication channels and platforms, as well as using targeted messaging, was vital for long-term engagement as content discovery driven by artificial intelligence reshapes digital outreach. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will embed newsletter growth within an integrated editorial strategy and leverage field office networks to amplify thematic advocacy content. This approach positions the subprogramme to adapt to evolving technologies and audience behaviours, ensuring resilience and relevance.
28.30	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.IV).
Figure 28.IV
Performance measure: number of newsletter subscribers on thematic priority issues (cumulative)
(Millions)

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – News services
Objective
28.32	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to mobilize audiences and strengthen support for the work, principles and purposes of the United Nations.
Strategy
28.33	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Provide the timely delivery of accurate, impartial, comprehensive, balanced, coherent and objective news and information;
(b)	Produce customized news and information materials (textual, audiovisual and photographic);

(c)	Provide services to the media and other consumers, including through the use of the newest web-based and mobile distribution platforms, with an overall emphasis on multilingualism from the planning stage.

28.34	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in increased public awareness of and engagement with the United Nations through access to more user-friendly and diversified content and a broader range of options available to audiences, from traditional to social media.
Programme performance in 2025
Increased public reach on United Nations news coverage

28.35	In 2025, UNifeed played a critical role in expanding global access to high-quality United Nations news content. As the video syndication service for more than 40 United Nations system partners, UNifeed supplied international broadcasters with timely coverage on climate action, peacekeeping, human rights, development issues and global economic and social conditions. It also provided essential reporting on major crises. UNifeed’s unique access to United Nations agencies and personnel on the ground enabled the production of exclusive material that became a primary content pipeline for leading global media organizations, further strengthened through its distribution partnerships with the Associated Press and Reuters. Beyond major broadcasters, UNifeed proved especially valuable for journalists and outlets with limited resources, offering free, reliable and ready-to-use video reporting that supported their ability to cover international developments. Outlets using UNifeed materials included major global networks such as Al Ghad Al Arabi, Al Jazeera, ABC Australia, BBC, CNN, CCTV, CNBC, Deutsche Welle, Euronews, France 24, Globo, RAI and TRT World, among many others, demonstrating both strong demand and extensive geographical reach. Monitoring data confirmed broad global distribution of usage by major media outlets, underscoring UNifeed’s continued role as a trusted, high-impact source of United Nations news content. The use of UNifeed material has been growing steadily in recent years. In 2025, the number of broadcasters using UNifeed material increased by 107 compared with the previous year, an increase of 5.35 per cent, and the largest annual increase over the past three years.
28.36	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.V).
Figure 28.V
Performance measure: number of broadcasters using UNifeed material (annual)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: deeper engagement of audiences through live-blogging tools and formats – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027
28.37	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 2,119 hours and 43 minutes of audience engagement time on live blogs, which exceeded the planned target of 350 hours.
28.38	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.VI).
Figure 28.VI
Performance measure: audience engagement time on live blogs (annual)
(Total time spent on page in hours)
Result 2: increased audience engagement and support for United Nations international observances – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

28.39	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 14.6 million views, which did not meet the planned target of 23 million views. The target was not met owing to a combination of factors, including changes in user search behaviour and the introduction of a new website audience analytics tracker in 2025, resulting in different data tracking. The implementation of the new analytics tracker in 2025 means that a comparison cannot be made between data gathered in 2024 (and previous years) and data gathered in 2025, given that the tool is counting different data sets.
28.40	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.VII).
Figure 28.VII
Performance measure: views of websites for international observances (annual)
(Millions)
Note: Data for 2023 are from 1 May to 31 December 2023. Planned targets for 2026 and 2027 reflect updated estimates based on the new website audience analytics tracker.
Result 3: enhanced and timely global access to United Nations News – Proposed programme plan for 2027

28.41	Prior to 2025, publication of content on the United Nations News website was limited to staff located at United Nations Headquarters in New York. To better serve global audiences across diverse time zones, the subprogramme sought to enhance its technical capability to upload content from duty stations beyond New York, to enable earlier publication of United Nations News content. This capability was rolled out to Geneva in 2025.
Lessons learned and planned change

28.42	The lesson learned was that enabling staff in Geneva to publish material directly to the United Nations News allowed new content to be posted outside New York working hours, thereby engaging global audiences with newly published stories closer to real time. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will expand this capability beyond Geneva and roll out the capacity to publish content from other duty stations, including Nairobi and Vienna.
28.43	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.VIII).
Figure 28.VIII
Performance measure: number of views on United Nations News website in all six official languages (annual)
(Millions)
Deliverables
28.44	Table 28.3 lists all deliverables of the subprogramme.
Table 28.3

D.	Communication deliverables

External and media relations: accreditation of and services to 5,100 members of the media (including 1,100 long-term grounds passes, 2,200 temporary passes and 1,800 requests for media passes from Member States); covering United Nations meetings and issuing press releases in the six official languages; coverage of intergovernmental meetings; online publication and distribution of speeches and statements of the Secretary-General and the Deputy Secretary-General and transcripts of press encounters of the Secretary-General; and photo coverage of meetings of key intergovernmental bodies and activities of the Secretary-General, the Deputy Secretary-General and the President of the General Assembly.

Digital platforms and multimedia content: daily multimedia content – audio, video and text – in the six official languages and four non-official languages (Hindi, Kiswahili, Portuguese and Urdu) disseminated through multiple platforms and channels, including online and on social media, and through partnerships with external media organizations; the core United Nations website (including the home page), 221 United Nations observances and major global campaigns in the six official languages; assistance to United Nations entities in the multilingual aspects of their websites (39 websites); live television coverage, including live streams, and the posting of on-demand webcasts of intergovernmental meetings and other newsworthy events taking place at Headquarters, including the Sustainable Development Goals Studio; and provision of audiovisual and photo library services to delegates, media outlets and others.

children.text: Subprogramme 3 – Outreach and knowledge services
Objective
28.45	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to mobilize audiences and strengthen support for the work, principles and purposes of the United Nations.
Strategy
28.46	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Engage in advocacy with a broad and international range of academic institutions, students and educators, the creative community and civil society;
(b)	Organize briefing programmes, special events, guided tours and interactive workshops, and engage with organizers of Model United Nations conferences;

(c)	Organize outreach and education programmes on the Holocaust and the transatlantic slave trade and create publications, research guides and exhibits on the same priorities, with a special focus on young people;
(d)	Develop communications materials, visual identities and branding in support of United Nations priorities;

(e)	Develop and maintain strategic partnerships with the entertainment, advertising and publishing industries, major sports and international events and institutions of higher education and research, targeting young people in particular;

(f)	Work with partners to develop multilingual and effective educational resources and programmes, campaigns, projects and events to inspire and encourage action for the Sustainable Development Goals;
(g)	Facilitate knowledge-sharing and the preservation of United Nations information resources for internal and external audiences.
28.47	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	A better understanding of the principles and purposes of the United Nations and the mobilization of young people and student participation in local, regional or national political, non-governmental and intergovernmental processes;

(b)	Increased mobilization of youth and student activity to advance the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals and foster a new generation of leaders committed to the principles and values of the United Nations;

(c)	Increased access to United Nations documentation and information by Member States, United Nations staff around the world, research institutions, young people, schools and the general public for improved engagement, awareness and decision-making.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened global support and action for the work of the United Nations

28.48	Under the theme “United for a Better Future”, the United Nations participated in Expo 2025 in Osaka, Japan, held from 13 April to 13 October 2025. The Department coordinated the United Nations pavilion, which showcased the work of 35 United Nations entities and 15 Secretariat departments and offices. Over six months, the United Nations pavilion welcomed 406,828 visitors and presented the Organization’s 80-year efforts in advancing peace and security, human rights, sustainable development and climate action. Through interactive installations, visitors also learned how the work of the Organization continued to benefit the Earth and populations around the world. As part of the visitor experience, a six-minute immersive film was screened that illustrated a vision for a peaceful, prosperous and sustainable future that can be achieved through collective action. In addition to the permanent exhibits, the pavilion also featured 24 rotating weekly exhibitions and 54 events across priority thematic areas, including the “United Nations Special Day”, on 22 August, with the participation of the Secretary-General. A visitor survey conducted at the pavilion, completed by more than 150,000 respondents, demonstrated a notable positive shift in visitors’ perceptions of the United Nations.
28.49	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 28.4).
Table 28.4
Performance measure

table_content.text: 2023 (actual)
2024 (actual)
2025 (actual)

A visitor survey completed by more than 150,000 respondents indicated a significant positive shift in public perceptions, with 96 per cent reporting an improved view of the United Nations and an equal proportion expressing motivation to take action towards a better future

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: young journalists gain access and insight into the work of the United Nations – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

28.50	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 17 journalists participating in the Reham Al‑Farra Memorial Journalists’ Fellowship Programme, in a virtual format, which exceeded the planned target of 12 fellows. The fellows produced 291 communication materials, including 71 stories published in their media outlets, and 220 social media posts, photo essays and video features. This exceeded the planned target of 185 stories.
28.51	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.IX).
Figure 28.IX
Performance measure: number of young journalists participating in the Programme and number of news stories produced by fellows (annual)
Result 2: advancing implementation of the Sustainable Development Goals through higher education institutions – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

28.52	The subprogramme’s work contributed to an additional 150 universities that became members of the United Nations Academic Impact network, for a cumulative total of 1,800 member universities, which exceeded the planned target of 1,700. Given the increase in the number of member universities achieved in 2025, the subprogramme will shift its focus from expanding the network to sustaining engagement with existing member universities, and therefore expects only a modest increase in the number of member universities by 2027.
28.53	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.X).
Figure 28.X
Performance measure: number of universities that are members of the United Nations Academic Impact network (cumulative)
Result 3: increased number of visitors to United Nations Headquarters through thematic guided tours – Proposed programme plan for 2027

28.54	Guided tours at United Nations Headquarters educate the public on global issues, foster understanding of the Organization’s work and offer a unique, multilingual perspective on international cooperation. Thematic guided tours were introduced during the coronavirus disease (COVID‑19) pandemic to broaden the portfolio of offerings available at a time when schools were closed and educators required additional online resources to engage and inform students. During the suspension of in‑person tour operations, tour guides and visitor services staff were able to devote increased time to the development of new content and visual materials. Initially launched in a virtual format, the thematic tours were subsequently adapted for in‑person delivery when operations resumed in April 2022. The current thematic tour portfolio encompasses curated tours on art, architecture, the garden, women’s contributions to the work of the United Nations, Black history and a dedicated children’s tour. Since 2023, all thematic tours have recorded a steady increase in participation.
Lessons learned and planned change

28.55	The lesson for the subprogramme was that diversifying the guided tour portfolio not only attracted new audiences with specific thematic interests, but also promoted repeat visits from clients seeking a different United Nations experience. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will promote and grow the popular thematic tours and explore the introduction of additional thematic tours on new themes.
28.56	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 28.XI).
Figure 28.XI
Performance measure: number of visitors to United Nations Headquarters in New York who took a thematic guided tour (annual)
